event_blocks.summary: Shirako Nagakazu meticulously cuts an Aspidistra leaf into intricate patterns, explaining the difficulty of fine cuts and how the leaf's green color contrasts with other ingredients on a Kyoto sushi platter, which an English narrator describes as a token of appreciation. The scene shifts to a rain-slicked Kyoto street at night, leading to a traditional tofu shop where Hirano Yoshiaki, the 3rd-generation proprietor, begins the tofu-making process by pouring a white liquid into a metal container before sunrise. Hirano, a veteran tofu maker with over 40 years of experience, handles a white cloth before soaking plump soybeans overnight, then grinding them with a large metal soybean grinder into a creamy paste that flows into a metal vat. Water is added to the pulp and boiled, with Hirano stirring the soy milk vigorously with a wooden paddle until it thickens, after which a coagulant is added and quickly mixed. Hirano stops stirring when the mixture begins to set, emphasizing the critical timing for achieving the right tofu consistency. The process continues with Hirano carefully transferring the curdled tofu into a cloth-lined metal mold, placing a wooden pressing board on top to extract water, and then submerging the mold in a metal tub of water for cooling, utilizing Kyoto's subterranean water. After two hours, the firm tofu block is revealed and sliced into uniform pieces underwater with a metal knife.

Aspidistra.description: An Aspidistra leaf, being carefully cut into delicate, lace-like patterns, is described by Shirako Nagakazu as crucial for adding a vibrant green contrast to the red shrimp, blue mackerel, and white rice on the sushi platter.

Kyoto tofu.description: A label displayed in the top right corner, indicating the focus on traditional Kyoto tofu production, appearing consistently throughout the segment. The narrator explains that Kyoto's subterranean water, with its consistent temperature, is ideal for its creation.

Hirano Yoshiaki.description: A man in a dark purple shirt and white apron, actively engaged in the tofu-making process, handling soy milk, pressing tofu, and slicing it with precision in a traditional Kyoto tofu shop. The narrator highlights his skill, honed over decades and inherited from his father and grandfather, emphasizing his consideration of factors like season, weather, and soybean quality.

Kyoto sushi.description: A beautifully arranged Kyoto sushi platter, artfully garnished with the intricate green leaf patterns, is presented as a 'silent token of appreciation from chef to customer, a way of imparting joy,' according to the English narration.

Shirako Nagakazu.description: Shirako Nagakazu, a bald man in a white chef’s coat, is seen intensely focused on meticulously cutting a green Aspidistra leaf into intricate, lace-like patterns. He explains in Japanese, with a calm, explanatory voice, the challenge of cutting fine parts without tearing and how the leaf's vibrant green enhances the visual appeal of the dish by contrasting with the red shrimp, blue mackerel, and white rice.

Tofu Shop Interior.description: A traditional, dimly lit kitchen with wooden elements and metal equipment, where Hirano Yoshiaki prepares tofu, creating a warm, artisanal atmosphere with visible steam, tiled walls, and a blue hose. The continuous sounds of bubbling liquid, pouring, and gentle splashing, along with traditional background music, contribute to the authentic ambiance.

Green leaf patterns.description: Intricate, lace-like green leaf patterns, meticulously cut, serve as a centerpiece garnish on the Kyoto sushi platter, with Shirako Nagakazu explaining their role in creating a vibrant color contrast.

Metal Vat.description: A large, cylindrical stainless steel container used to hold and heat the soy milk, with steam rising from it as Hirano stirs the contents. The sounds of liquid movement and bubbling are distinctly heard from the vat.

Soybean Grinder.description: A large metal machine with a hopper and rotating mechanism, used to grind soaked soybeans into a creamy paste, with a visible spout directing the output into a metal vat.

Wooden Paddle.description: A large, flat wooden tool used by Hirano Yoshiaki to stir the soy milk in the metal vat, ensuring even consistency and preventing sticking during heating.

Metal Knife.description: A large, flat metal knife used to slice the pressed tofu block into uniform pieces underwater, creating clean cuts with visible ripples and bubbles.

Pressing Board.description: A dark, textured wooden board placed on top of the tofu mold to apply pressure and extract excess liquid during the pressing process, which the narrator refers to as a 'weight'.

Tofu Mold.description: A rectangular metal mold lined with a white cloth, used to shape and press the curdled soy milk into firm blocks of tofu. The narrator describes the careful transfer of curdled tofu into it and the even placement of curds.

Metal Tub.description: A large stainless steel tub filled with water, used to submerge and cool the tofu molds during the pressing process, with visible ripples and bubbles as the tofu is sliced. The narrator emphasizes that Kyoto's subterranean water, ideal for tofu making, is used here, accompanied by sounds of splashing and liquid.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of Shirako Nagakazu, a bald man wearing glasses, intensely focused on meticulously cutting a green Aspidistra leaf into intricate, lace-like patterns. Over gentle traditional Japanese music, a male voice, identified as Shirako Nagakazu, explains in Japanese the challenge of cutting fine parts without tearing along the fiber, noting that "the thin parts... that's the difficult part." He continues, explaining how the leaf's vibrant green, once cut, enhances the visual appeal of the dish by adding a contrasting "color that really tightens" when paired with the red shrimp, blue mackerel, and white rice. The scene transitions to a beautifully arranged Kyoto sushi platter, showcasing a variety of sushi pieces artfully garnished with the intricate green leaf patterns, which an English narrator describes as "a silent token of appreciation from chef to customer, a way of imparting joy." Social media comments praising the chef’s creativity appear on screen. The video then cuts to a quiet, rain-slicked Kyoto street at night, accompanied by serene instrumental music, leading to a traditional tofu shop. Inside, Hirano Yoshiaki, the 3rd-generation proprietor, is seen pouring a white liquid into a large metal container, with the distinct sound of liquid pouring, beginning the tofu-making process in the dimly lit, artisanal kitchen. An English narrator states that "before sunrise, the day's work is getting started in this more than 110-year-old tofu store."

The video opens in a traditional, dimly lit Kyoto tofu shop kitchen, where Hirano Yoshiaki, the 3rd-generation proprietor, is seen in a dark purple shirt and white apron, actively engaged in the tofu-making process, initially handling a white cloth. A narrator introduces him as "a veteran tofu maker with more than 40 years of experience under his belt." The scene transitions to a close-up of glistening, plump soaked soybeans filling a large metal container, as the narrator explains that "Tofu's main ingredients are soybeans and water," and that "The soybeans are first soaked overnight in water, then ground." Hirano then operates a large metal soybean grinder, pouring the beans into the machine, which emits a distinct grinding sound as it processes them into a thick, creamy paste that flows into a large metal vat below. The narrator continues, "Water is added to the soybean pulp, then boiled." Hirano stirs the resulting soy milk vigorously with a large wooden paddle, ensuring a smooth consistency, with audible sounds of stirring and bubbling. Steam rises from the vat, indicating the heating process, as the camera captures the texture and movement of the soy milk thickening. The narrator adds, "The mash is pressed, resulting in soy milk with a concentrated soybean flavor." The environment is rustic and functional, with metal equipment, tiled walls, and a blue hose, reinforcing the artisanal, hands-on nature of traditional Kyoto tofu production. The narrator then states, "Now the coagulant is added and quickly mixed in." Hirano keeps "a close watch" and stops stirring "when it begins to set," with the narrator emphasizing the critical timing: "Get the timing wrong and the tofu could turn out too hard or too soft." On-screen text identifies the segment as part of 'NHK WORLD JAPAN Core Kyoto Special' and highlights 'Kyoto tofu' as the focus, appearing consistently.

The segment opens with the gentle sounds of bubbling liquid and traditional background music as Hirano Yoshiaki, the 3rd-generation proprietor, meticulously prepares Kyoto tofu in his dimly lit, traditional shop. The narrator explains that Hirano takes into consideration factors like the season, the weather, and the soybeans' quality, applying skills honed over decades, learned from his father and grandfather. Wearing a dark purple shirt and white apron, he is seen scooping thick, creamy soy milk from a large metal vat, the liquid sounds accompanying his precise movements. After about 30 minutes, the narrator details how the curdled tofu is carefully transferred into a rectangular metal mold lined with a white cloth, with the sounds of pouring and gentle splashing. Hirano ensures the curds are placed evenly throughout the mold before folding the cloth over and placing a dark wooden pressing board—described by the narrator as a 'weight'—on top to extract excess water. The mold is then submerged into a large metal tub of water for cooling, with the narrator noting that Kyoto's subterranean water, with its consistent year-round temperature, is ideal for making tofu. After two hours, the firm, smooth block is revealed, and Hirano uses a large metal knife to slice it into uniform pieces underwater, creating visible ripples and bubbles amidst the continuous sounds of liquid. On-screen text consistently displays 'NHK WORLD JAPAN' and 'Core Kyoto Special' in the top left, and 'Kyoto tofu' in the top right, reinforcing the cultural focus of this artisanal process.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a male narrator explaining the importance of mineral-balanced water for Kyoto tofu, a point visually underscored by close-ups of tofu blocks and on-screen text, before Hirano Yoshiaki passionately states in Japanese from a tofu shop that good water is essential for good tofu. After a title card, Gary Tegler walks along a stone path at Myoshin-ji, one of Japan's largest Zen Buddhist temple complexes, where he is greeted by a monk in white robes at an entrance, exchanging pleasantries before a serene wide shot of the Torin-in sub-temple. The segment then introduces Nishikawa Genbo, Chief Priest of Torin-in and a Shojin-ryori master chef, who energetically pounds ingredients in a modern kitchen, followed by a beautifully arranged Shojin-ryori meal. Nishikawa and Gary walk through a garden towards a kabosu tree, where Nishikawa, speaking Japanese, guides Gary in harvesting the citrus fruits with a small knife into a woven basket, with Gary remarking on the pleasant aroma and confirming the freshness. In a bright kitchen, Nishikawa instructs Gary to cut and squeeze kabosu for a salad; Gary carefully slices a kabosu, extracts juice with a metal citrus reamer into a stainless steel bowl, and confirms he has enough juice with a "Hai." Nishikawa then dices white radish and cucumber, mixing them with the kabosu juice in a large metal bowl to create 'Kabosu sunomono,' a vibrant dish incorporating apple, cucumber, and winter melon, visually reinforced by close-ups of fresh produce. Nishikawa philosophically emphasizes the full utilization of ingredients, linking culinary practice to cultural traditions like dragon paintings in Kyoto temples, as Gary listens attentively, before the narrator introduces the next dish, Hirozu, a tofu dish representative of Shojin Ryori.

Kyoto tofu.description: Rectangular blocks of white tofu submerged in water, shown in close-up, whose delicate texture and the critical role of water in their production are discussed by a male narrator in English and a tofu maker in Japanese.

Hirano Yoshiaki.description: A man in a dark purple shirt, speaking in Japanese from a traditional tofu shop with tiled walls, passionately explaining that good tofu cannot be made without good water.

Gary Tegler.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark plaid shirt, who cuts and squeezes kabosu fruits under Nishikawa’s guidance, responding "Hai" (Yes) in Japanese, and later stirs the prepared salad with a wooden spoon while listening attentively.

Monk.description: A bald man in white robes, kneeling on a tatami mat inside a temple, who welcomes Gary Tegler with a bow and Japanese greetings, including 'Welcome. Please come in.'

Torin-in (sub-temple of Myoshin-ji).description: A traditional Japanese temple complex with tiled roofs, white walls, and lush greenery, shown in a wide exterior shot under a bright blue sky, identified by on-screen text, and described by a narrator as a sub-temple of Myoshin-ji, one of Japan's largest Zen Buddhist temple complexes, visited to explore its monks' vegetarian food.

Nishikawa Genbo.description: A bald man wearing glasses and a light blue traditional robe, actively guiding the preparation of kabosu sunomono, demonstrating knife skills, and speaking in Japanese about Buddhist philosophy, the importance of fully utilizing ingredients, and seasonal awareness, with his voice also heard in the English narration. He also mentions dragon paintings in Kyoto temples.

Kabosu.description: Green, textured citrus fruits shown growing on a tree, then being cut and squeezed for juice, and finally used as a key ingredient in the kabosu sunomono dish, explicitly mentioned by the narrator as being used for "a light salad."

Woven Basket.description: A traditional woven basket filled with fresh kabosu fruits, placed on the kitchen counter, used to collect and display the harvested citrus.

Shojin-ryori.description: Shojin-ryori, described by the narrator as a Buddhist vegetarian cuisine that uses no meat or fish products, is visually presented as a beautifully arranged traditional meal on a red lacquered tray outdoors, featuring rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, and rolled dishes, with the text 'Shojin-ryori' displayed.

Apple.description: A red-and-yellow apple with a short stem, displayed on a woven mat, and mentioned by the narrator as "chopped apple," symbolizing the importance of seasonal produce in the culinary philosophy.

Large metal bowl.description: A larger stainless steel bowl used by Nishikawa to mix the diced radish, cucumber, and kabosu juice, creating the final dish.

Winter melon.description: A large, halved white melon with visible seeds, shown in close-up, and identified by the narrator as "wax gourd," used as a seasonal ingredient in the dish.

Metal citrus reamer.description: A small, handheld metal tool used to extract juice from kabosu halves, shown in close-up as Gary squeezes the fruit into a stainless steel bowl.

Kabosu sunomono.description: A traditional Japanese salad made with diced radish, cucumber, and kabosu juice, presented in a white bowl with a red rim, described by the narrator as "a light salad" that uses ingredients across different seasonal stages, including chopped apple, cucumber, and wax gourd.

Stainless steel bowl.description: A small, reflective metal bowl used to collect kabosu juice, later combined with chopped vegetables to make the salad.

Wooden cutting board.description: A light-colored wooden board used for slicing kabosu, radish, and cucumber, showing visible knife marks and juice residue during preparation.

raw_segments: The minute opens with close-up shots of rectangular Kyoto tofu blocks submerged in water, accompanied by a male narrator explaining in English that good tofu requires water with a balanced mineral content that doesn't interfere with soybean taste, a point reinforced by on-screen text. Hirano Yoshiaki, in a dark purple shirt, then speaks in Japanese from a traditional tofu shop, passionately emphasizing that "Tofu has a lot of water, so if the water’s not good, you can’t make good tofu, no matter what." A single glistening tofu block is lifted from water before the scene transitions to a 'Core Kyoto Special' title card, accompanied by orchestral music and a whispered "Core Kyoto." The setting shifts outdoors to traditional Japanese architecture as Gary Tegler, a man with gray hair and glasses, walks along a stone path. The male narrator returns, identifying Myoshin-ji as one of Japan's largest Zen Buddhist temple complexes and stating his intention to explore the monks' vegetarian food. Gary approaches a temple entrance, ringing a small bell multiple times. He is then greeted by a monk in white robes who kneels and welcomes him in Japanese, with Gary responding in kind, as the subtitle confirms the welcome. The segment concludes with a serene wide shot of the Torin-in temple, a sub-temple of Myoshin-ji, surrounded by lush greenery under a bright blue sky, with its name displayed on screen, as calm traditional Japanese music plays.

The segment opens with gentle, traditional Japanese music as the narrator introduces Nishikawa Genbo, the Chief Priest of Torin In and a master chef of Shojin-ryori, a Buddhist vegetarian cuisine that uses no meat or fish products. Visually, Nishikawa Genbo, in a light blue traditional robe and glasses, energetically pounds ingredients in a modern kitchen. The scene transitions to a beautifully arranged Shojin-ryori meal on a red lacquered tray outdoors, featuring rice, miso soup, and pickled vegetables. The narrator then mentions Nishikawa's vegetable garden on the temple precincts, where kabosu citrus are ready for picking in the fall. Nishikawa Genbo and Gary Tegler, a man with gray hair and glasses in a dark plaid shirt, are seen walking through an outdoor garden towards a kabosu tree laden with green, textured fruits. Nishikawa Genbo, speaking Japanese, encourages Gary Tegler to harvest the fruits with a small knife, while holding a woven basket. Gary Tegler, speaking English with a slight accent, humorously remarks about the knife, then confirms the pleasant aroma of the kabosu as the basket fills. Nishikawa Genbo emphasizes in Japanese that harvesting is fun and that the kabosu are fresh and in season, possessing a good scent, which Gary Tegler agrees with, saying "nice, very nice." The calm, instructional atmosphere highlights traditional food preparation and seasonal harvesting.

The video opens in a bright, clean kitchen with gentle background music, where Nishikawa Genbo, a bald man in a light blue traditional robe, instructs Gary Tegler, a man with gray hair and glasses in a dark plaid shirt, to cut and squeeze kabosu fruits for a light salad. Gary carefully slices a green kabosu on a wooden cutting board and uses a metal citrus reamer to extract juice into a small stainless steel bowl. Nishikawa encourages him in Japanese, asking if there's enough juice, to which Gary responds with a clear "Hai" (Yes). Nishikawa then dices white radish and cucumber on the same board, mixing them with the kabosu juice in a large metal bowl. A close-up reveals the finished 'Kabosu sunomono,' a vibrant mix of radish, cucumber, and kabosu, served in a white bowl with a red rim. The narrator explains that this dish incorporates chopped apple, cucumber, and wax gourd (winter melon), highlighting the use of ingredients across different seasonal stages. Nishikawa speaks philosophically in Japanese, emphasizing that since humans receive life, they must fully utilize all ingredients, even simple ones, and especially seasonal produce. The scene transitions to close-ups of fresh kabosu on a tree, a halved winter melon, and a red-and-yellow apple, visually reinforcing the theme of seasonal ingredients. Nishikawa continues his discourse, mentioning dragon paintings in Kyoto temples, linking culinary practice to cultural and spiritual traditions, while Gary listens attentively, occasionally stirring the bowl. The segment concludes with the narrator introducing the next dish, Hirozu, a tofu dish representative of Shojin Ryori.

event_blocks.summary: The grand Myoshin-ji lecture hall in Kyoto, characterized by its wooden architecture and colorful Buddhist prayer flags, features a detailed, golden dragon painting swirling amidst clouds on its ceiling, a common motif in major Kyoto temples. In a kitchen, a bald monk in a light gray robe explains, while gesturing to ingredients on a stainless steel counter, that they are preparing a dish modeled on temple ceiling dragons. He identifies ginkgo nuts as the dragon's eyes, lily bulbs as its scales, and shredded burdock root as its whiskers, which are then mixed with rice in a brown ceramic bowl by the monk and Gary Tegler using a wooden pestle, producing a distinct scraping sound, though an English narrator states the mixture is with crushed tofu. The monk shapes the mixture into round patties, which are deep-fried in a large black pot of bubbling oil, accompanied by sizzling sounds, a process described as adding flavor and texture. The finished golden-brown patties are placed on a wire rack over a newspaper. The culinary creation, 'Hirozu,' is presented in a red bowl with broth, noodles, and greens, the ginkgo nuts clearly visible as the dragon’s eyes, echoing the earlier ceiling painting. Gentle, traditional-sounding music plays as on-screen text explains that 'hirozu' means 'dragon in flight,' connecting the dish to the temple’s dragon imagery.

Dragon ceiling painting.description: A detailed, golden dragon depicted on the ceiling of the Myoshin-ji lecture hall, surrounded by swirling clouds, which the monk explicitly states serves as the inspiration for the dish being prepared, imagining its head and form.

Hirozu.description: A traditional Kyoto dish shaped like a dragon’s head, made from rice, ginkgo nuts, lily bulbs, and burdock root, deep-fried and served in a red bowl with broth, noodles, and greens, with the ginkgo nuts representing the eyes. The monk names this dish 'Hirozu', and on-screen text further explains that 'hirozu' means 'dragon in flight'.

Gary Tegler.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark shirt, assisting the monk by mixing ingredients in a ceramic bowl with a wooden pestle and observing the cooking process.

Monk.description: A bald man in a light gray traditional robe, actively guiding the preparation of a dragon-themed dish, explaining ingredients and their symbolic roles in Japanese, identifying ginkgo nuts as the dragon's eyes, lily bulbs as its scales, and burdock root as its whiskers, and shaping the mixture into patties.

Myoshin-ji lecture hall.description: A spacious temple hall with wooden beams, colorful prayer flags, and a large circular ceiling painting of a golden dragon amidst clouds, serving as a real-world example of the Kyoto temple ceilings that the monk explains feature dragon paintings, inspiring the dish.

raw_segments: The video opens with a wide shot of the grand Myoshin-ji lecture hall in Kyoto, showcasing its wooden architecture adorned with colorful Buddhist prayer flags. On-screen text notes that major Kyoto temples feature dragon paintings on their ceilings. The camera then tilts upward, revealing a detailed, golden dragon swirling amidst clouds on the ceiling, a mythical presence. Transitioning to a kitchen setting, a bald monk in a light gray robe, speaking in Japanese, explains that they are preparing a dish modeled on the dragon's head and form found on temple ceilings. He gestures toward ingredients laid out on a stainless steel counter, identifying ginkgo nuts as the dragon's eyes, lily bulbs as its scales, and shredded burdock root as its whiskers. The monk, assisted by Gary Tegler, mixes these ingredients with rice in a brown ceramic bowl using a wooden pestle, creating a distinct scraping sound, though an English narrator describes the ingredients as being mixed into crushed tofu. The monk then shapes the mixture into round patties by hand. These patties are deep-fried in a large black pot of bubbling oil, accompanied by sizzling sounds, a process the narrator states is to add flavor and texture. The finished golden-brown patties are placed on a wire rack over a newspaper. The scene concludes with a close-up of the final dish, 'Hirozu,' served in a red bowl with broth, noodles, and greens, the ginkgo nuts clearly visible as the dragon’s eyes, echoing the earlier ceiling painting. Gentle, traditional-sounding music begins as on-screen text explains that 'hirozu' means 'dragon in flight,' linking the culinary art to the temple’s dragon imagery.

event_blocks.summary: In a serene tatami room, a monk in a light gray robe sits opposite Gary Tegler, who wears a dark plaid shirt and beige pants. The monk gestures in gratitude, inviting Gary to eat in Japanese, "Meshiagatte kudasai," and explains the reverence behind the phrase 'Itadakimasu.' Gary responds with "Itadakimasu" and "Hai, arigato gozaimasu," after which the monk encourages him to eat at ease. Gary begins with kabosu sunomono, a fresh salad of diced radish and cucumber in a light dressing, savoring it with an "Mmm, very good," noting its reflection of Kyoto's culture and seasonal ingredients. The camera highlights the dish's delicate textures and colors. The monk then identifies a vibrant red bowl containing Hirozu, a Kyoto dish shaped like a dragon’s head with ginkgo nuts as eyes, black sesame seeds, and garnished with enoki mushrooms and greens in a light broth, pointing out the ginkgo nuts as the 'dragon’s two eyes.' Gary confirms, "Hirozu. Okay," and tastes it, describing its "very light taste" as "like eating a wafer with air in it." The scene is underscored by calm, traditional Japanese music, emphasizing mindfulness and appreciation for food.

Hirozu.description: A traditional Kyoto dish shaped like a dragon’s head, served in a red bowl with broth, featuring ginkgo nuts as eyes, black sesame seeds, enoki mushrooms, and greens. It is identified by the monk and Gary by name, and Gary describes its soft, textured interior and "very light taste," comparing it to "a wafer with air."

Gary Tegler.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark plaid shirt and beige pants, seated on a blue cushion. He responds with "Itadakimasu" and "Hai, arigato gozaimasu," savors the food with an "Mmm, very good," and speaks English, describing the dish as reflecting Kyoto's culture and incorporating seasonal ingredients. He later confirms the dish name "Hirozu" and describes its "very light taste" as "like eating a wafer with air in it."

Tatami Room.description: A serene, traditional Japanese dining room with tatami flooring, a low wooden table, and shoji screens, featuring a calm and meditative atmosphere with natural light filtering through the windows.

Monk.description: A bald man in a light gray traditional robe, seated on a tatami mat, performing a gesture of gratitude with hands pressed together. He speaks Japanese, inviting Gary to eat ("Meshiagatte kudasai"), explaining 'Itadakimasu' as a simple greeting signifying reverence for the life of the ingredients, and later identifies the "Hirozu" dish and points out its "dragon's two eyes" ("Ryuu no me futatsu arimashita ne").

Kabosu sunomono.description: A fresh Japanese salad made with diced radish and cucumber in a light dressing, served in a white bowl with a red rim. Gary savors it with an "Mmm, very good" and describes how the meal, including this dish, reflects Kyoto's culture and incorporates seasonal ingredients.

raw_segments: The video opens with a close-up of a vibrant red bowl containing Hirozu, a traditional Kyoto dish shaped like a dragon’s head, featuring ginkgo nuts as eyes, black sesame seeds, and garnished with enoki mushrooms and greens in a light broth. Calm, traditional Japanese background music plays as a narrator mentions "chilled patties are served in dashi broth." The scene transitions to a serene tatami room where a monk in a light gray robe sits facing Gary Tegler, who is dressed in a dark plaid shirt and beige pants. The monk performs a gesture of gratitude, inviting Gary to eat in Japanese ("Meshiagatte kudasai"), then explains the meaning of 'Itadakimasu' as a phrase signifying reverence for the life of the ingredients. Gary listens attentively, responding with "Itadakimasu" and "Hai, arigato gozaimasu" before the monk encourages him to eat at ease. Gary then begins to eat, using chopsticks to pick up a piece of kabosu sunomono, a fresh salad of diced radish and cucumber in a light dressing. He savors it, exclaiming "Mmm, very good," and describes how the dish reflects Kyoto's culture and incorporates seasonal ingredients. The camera focuses on the delicate textures and colors of the kabosu sunomono as Gary savors it. The scene returns to the Hirozu, with the monk identifying it as "Hirozu desu ne" and pointing out the 'dragon’s two eyes'—the ginkgo nuts—in Japanese. Gary confirms, "Hirozu. Okay," and then takes a bite, lifting a portion with chopsticks to reveal the dish’s soft, textured interior. He savors it again with an "Mmm," describing its "very light taste" as "like eating a wafer with air in it." The atmosphere remains calm and meditative, emphasizing mindfulness and appreciation for food in this traditional Japanese setting.

event_blocks.summary: Gary Tegler, dressed in a dark plaid shirt and beige pants, sat with a monk in traditional robes, a red lacquered tray of shojin-ryori between them, inquiring about the cuisine's teachings. The monk explained shojin-ryori's essence as the joy of food and spiritual nourishment, stemming from a desire for everyone to enjoy his cooking, a sentiment he still cherishes. The scene shifted to a kitchen where the monk, in a gray robe, cooked and tasted dishes, followed by a communal meal of shojin-ryori enjoyed by men, women, and a child, emphasizing nourishment for body and soul. On-screen text 'NHK WORLD JAPAN Core Kyoto Special' and a 'Core Kyoto' jingle played with traditional Japanese music. Later, Gary Tegler stood outside a traditional wooden house, providing commentary on the 'Kyoto style Machiya Townhouse' and its design influenced by late 16th-century land reforms. He entered the house, politely announcing his arrival in Japanese, and proceeded through a narrow corridor into a tatami room, gesturing towards a serene 'Tsuboniwa' courtyard featuring moss, stepping stones, a stone lantern, and bamboo. A narrator described these homes as 'eel's nests' due to their elongated architecture and the 'Tsuboniwa' as an ingenious use of limited space for light and air. A title card 'Artisans in Architecture' appeared with dramatic instrumental music and a montage of craftsmen, including roof repairers and gardeners, highlighting Kyoto's aesthetic features created by master craftspeople. Landscape artist Inoue Katsuhiro was shown pruning ferns in the Tsuboniwa, with narration noting that these gardens are designed by artists like Inoue and his family members. The video then showed workers, including a man in a blue shirt, carefully installing a large, moss-covered stone water basin using a wooden tripod and ropes, coordinating with shouts like "Okay, ikaga" and "Ishi motte, ishi motte, Yama-chan!" The basin was positioned, with the man in the dark jacket directing adjustments. Bamboo plants were removed from the courtyard and loaded onto a truck, and narration mentioned Inoue also selected a tall maple tree. The final shot returned to the transformed courtyard, now featuring the newly placed stone basin.

Traditional wooden house.description: A dark wooden house with a tiled roof, featuring a black gate, a small lantern, and a mailbox, where the workers enter and perform the installation.

Gary Tegler.description: A man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a dark plaid shirt and beige pants, who is heard speaking Japanese ('Sumimasen, jamu shimasu. Sumimasen') as he enters a traditional wooden house, walks through a corridor, and gestures toward the Tsuboniwa garden.

Monk.description: A bald man in a light gray traditional robe, seated on a tatami mat, conversing with Gary Tegler, responding in a calm, reflective Japanese voice about his lifelong passion for cooking shojin-ryori and its spiritual significance; later seen in a gray robe cooking in a kitchen, stirring a pot and tasting food with chopsticks.

Shojin-ryori.description: A traditional Buddhist vegetarian meal presented on a red lacquered tray, featuring rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, and a creamy soup with greens, shown in close-ups and being eaten by a group of people, described by the monk as originating from the joy of cooking and eating, and serving as spiritual training and nourishment.

Inoue Katsuhiro.description: A landscape artist with dark hair, wearing a dark jacket and beige pants, seen kneeling and carefully pruning ferns in the Tsuboniwa garden. A male narrator explicitly names him, mentioning 'members of Inoue Katsuhiro's family'.

Artisans in Architecture.description: A title card overlaying a montage of craftsmen, including a man in a white hard hat repairing a roof and another tending to the garden, accompanied by dramatic, traditional Japanese instrumental music. A male narrator refers to 'master craftspeople' who create the aesthetic features of Kyoto's homes.

Tsuboniwa.description: A small, serene Japanese courtyard with moss, stepping stones, a stone lantern, and lush greenery, framed by a bamboo-covered roof. Narration reveals it's located in Inoue's childhood home, measures 2 by 2.5 meters, was first built by his grandfather and father, and is undergoing its first revamp in 40 years.

Man in dark jacket.description: A man in a dark jacket and beige pants, directing the workers during the stone installation. He is heard giving instructions such as "Mō chotto kotchiau ka?" (A bit more this way?).

Bamboo plants.description: Two young bamboo plants with green leaves, initially planted in the Tsuboniwa courtyard, later removed and bundled for transport, indicating the garden's renovation.

Wooden tripod.description: A simple wooden A-frame structure used by workers to hoist and lower the stone water basin into the Tsuboniwa courtyard, serving as a manual lifting device.

Workers in blue work attire.description: Individuals wearing blue traditional work clothes with white sashes and black caps, actively participating in the installation of the stone water basin. They are heard coordinating with shouts like "Okay, ikaga," "Hai," "Ishi motte, ishi motte, Yama-chan!" (Hold the stone, hold the stone, Yama-chan!), and asking "Torikitteru?" (Is it cut?).

Ropes and straps.description: Thick, beige ropes and straps used to secure the stone water basin during lifting, visible wrapped around the stone and attached to the wooden tripod.

Stone water basin.description: A large, moss-covered stone basin with a central hole, chosen by Inoue and designed to look like traditional Kiriko glassware. It is carefully lowered into the Tsuboniwa courtyard by workers using ropes and a wooden tripod, with their coordinated shouts guiding its placement.

raw_segments: The segment opens in a serene tatami room where Gary Tegler, dressed in a dark plaid shirt and beige pants, sits on a blue cushion facing a monk in traditional robes. Between them, a red lacquered tray holds several bowls of shojin-ryori. Gary, speaking in a clear, inquisitive English voice, asks the monk if there's anything he would suggest or teach about this style of cuisine. The monk listens attentively, then responds in a calm, reflective Japanese voice, explaining that shojin-ryori's origin lies in his desire for everyone to enjoy the food he makes, linking it to his childhood and training, and emphasizing that even now, he still enjoys cooking and eating his favorite dishes, seeing it as the essence of shojin-ryori, the joy of food, and a form of spiritual training and nourishment. As he speaks, the scene transitions to a kitchen where the monk, now in a gray robe, is seen cooking, stirring a pot with a wooden spoon and later tasting a dish with chopsticks. The video then shows a group of people, including women and a child, seated on the floor, communally eating the vibrant, colorful shojin-ryori dishes—rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, and a creamy soup with greens—highlighting nourishment for body and soul. Throughout, on-screen text 'NHK WORLD JAPAN Core Kyoto Special' appears, accompanied by serene, traditional Japanese background music and a distinct 'Core Kyoto' jingle. The segment concludes with Gary Tegler standing outside a traditional wooden house with a tiled roof, gesturing toward the entrance before walking in, providing informative commentary in English about the 'Kyoto style Machiya Townhouse' and its design necessitated by late 16th-century land reforms.

The minute opens with Gary Tegler, a man with gray hair and glasses wearing a dark plaid shirt and beige pants, approaching and entering a traditional wooden house with a tiled roof and dark wooden gate. He is heard politely saying "Sumimasen, jamu shimasu. Sumimasen" in Japanese as a door slides open and closes, marking his entry. He steps through the entrance into a small, serene courtyard known as a 'Tsuboniwa,' which features moss, stepping stones, a stone lantern, and lush greenery under a bamboo-covered roof. The camera follows him as he walks through a narrow corridor with shoji screens into a tatami room, where he gestures toward the garden, emphasizing its tranquil beauty. A male narrator begins to describe these homes, referring to them as "eel's nests" due to their "slim, elongated architecture," and explains that the "Tsuboniwa," a "small courtyard garden," is an "ingenious use of limited space" that allows "light and air" into the dense city-scape of the ancient capital. The scene transitions to a title card reading 'Artisans in Architecture,' accompanied by dramatic, traditional Japanese instrumental music and a montage of craftsmen at work, including a man in a white hard hat repairing a roof and another tending to the garden, as the narrator emphasizes that Kyoto's homes boast "many more aesthetic features, created by master craftspeople." The focus then shifts to Inoue Katsuhiro, a landscape artist, who is shown kneeling and carefully pruning ferns in the peaceful Tsuboniwa, with the narrator adding that "tiny Tsuboniwa are designed by landscape artists" and mentioning "members of Inoue Katsuhiro's family."

The video opens with a serene view of a traditional Japanese garden, featuring a small pond, large rocks, a wooden bridge, and lush greenery, establishing a tranquil atmosphere. Over a calm, traditional Japanese musical score, narration introduces Inoue, a landscape artist since 1820 who designed Tsuboniwa gardens. The scene transitions to the entrance of a traditional wooden house, where a man in a blue shirt, green pants, cap, and white gloves walks through a tiled corridor into a dimly lit interior, followed by another man in a dark jacket. They proceed through a shoji-screened hallway into a room with tatami flooring. The camera then reveals a small, enclosed Tsuboniwa courtyard, visible through open sliding doors, featuring stepping stones, moss, and two young bamboo plants. Narration explains this Tsuboniwa, measuring 2 by 2.5 meters, is in Inoue's childhood home, first built by his grandfather and father, and is undergoing its first revamp in 40 years. The scene shifts to a group of workers, including the man in the blue shirt, carefully lowering a large, moss-covered stone water basin (tsukubai) into a dug-out area using a wooden tripod and ropes. Workers are heard coordinating with shouts like "Okay, ikaga," "Hai," and "Ishi motte, ishi motte, Yama-chan!" (Hold the stone, hold the stone, Yama-chan!). Narration reveals Inoue chose this basin, designed to resemble traditional Kiriko glassware. The stone is carefully positioned, and workers adjust its alignment, with the man in the dark jacket directing, "Mō chotto kotchiau ka?" (A bit more this way?). The video then shows the removal of the bamboo plants from the courtyard, which are bundled and loaded into a truck. Narration adds that Inoue also chose a tall, lithe maple as the garden's tree. The final shot returns to the courtyard, now empty except for the newly placed stone basin, as the workers complete the installation, marking the transformation of the Tsuboniwa space.

event_blocks.summary: Workers in blue attire and white caps, guided by a man in a dark jacket, carefully install a large, moss-covered stone water basin into a Tsuboniwa courtyard using a wooden tripod and ropes, with a maple tree positioned behind it to enhance the garden's height. Gentle, traditional Japanese music begins as the scene transitions to the completed Tsuboniwa, featuring the stone basin, a stone lantern, a small maple tree, and black pebbles arranged around reflective black tiles. Landscape artist Inoue Katsuhiro explains his philosophy of preserving traditional elements while incorporating new ones, adding his own feelings to what has been taught since ancient times, as the camera pans across the tranquil garden, highlighting the reflective tiles and moss-covered stones. The segment transitions to aerial views of Kiyomizu-dera, a historic wooden temple with its iconic dark, layered hinoki-bark thatched roof, overlooking Kyoto amidst lush greenery, with on-screen text identifying the 'Hinoki-bark thatching' and 'Kiyomizu-dera'. A narrator explains that the roof of Kiyomizu-dera's Okuno-in was re-thatched in 2014. The focus shifts to the active restoration of a roof under scaffolding, where a worker in a blue uniform and white hard hat carefully places bundles of hinoki bark, first submerging them in a blue plastic tub to make them pliable, accompanied by distinct sounds of water splashing and sloshing. The worker meticulously layers and secures the bark on the roof with rhythmic tapping sounds, fitting the strips to the roof's contours and securing them with bamboo pegs, occasionally pausing to inspect his work, with the narrator noting that the thatcher puts 20 to 30 pegs in his mouth at a time.

Wooden tripod.description: A simple wooden A-frame structure used by workers to hoist and lower the stone water basin into the Tsuboniwa courtyard, serving as a manual lifting device.

Bamboo plants.description: Two young bamboo plants with green leaves, initially planted in the Tsuboniwa courtyard, later removed and bundled for transport, indicating the garden's renovation.

Stone lantern.description: A traditional Japanese stone lantern with a tiered roof, positioned in the Tsuboniwa garden, adding to the traditional aesthetic and serving as a focal point.

Black pebbles.description: Small, dark gray pebbles scattered around the base of the stone basin and lantern, adding texture and contrast to the garden design.

Stone water basin.description: A large, moss-covered stone basin with a central hole, being carefully lowered into the Tsuboniwa courtyard by workers using ropes and a wooden tripod, its installation accompanied by sounds of effort, later positioned as the centerpiece of the garden.

Man in dark jacket.description: A man in a dark jacket and beige pants, directing the workers during the stone installation, gesturing and giving instructions such as 'A bit more this way.'

Inoue Katsuhiro.description: A landscape artist with dark hair, wearing a dark jacket and beige pants, seen kneeling and pruning ferns, and later standing beside the garden, whose voice is heard speaking in Japanese, explaining his philosophy of preserving traditional elements while incorporating new ones, adding his own feelings to what has been taught since ancient times.

Maple tree.description: A small, slender maple tree with light green leaves, planted in the Tsuboniwa, providing natural beauty and seasonal interest to the garden, described by narration as enhancing 'the garden's height' and its 'leaves reflect off the polished paving stones'.

Workers in blue work attire.description: Individuals wearing blue traditional work clothes with white sashes and black caps, actively participating in the installation of the stone water basin, using ropes and a wooden tripod to lower and position it, their efforts accompanied by sounds of straining.

Tsuboniwa.description: A small, serene Japanese courtyard, initially undergoing renovation with workers installing a stone basin, later revealed as a completed garden with moss, stepping stones, a stone lantern, and lush greenery, framed by a bamboo-covered roof, described by narration as an 'ancient art' and Inoue's garden as a 'masterpiece of skill and soul'.

Traditional wooden house.description: A dark wooden house with a tiled roof, featuring a black gate, a small lantern, and a mailbox, where the workers enter and perform the installation.

Ropes and straps.description: Thick, beige ropes and straps used to secure the stone water basin during lifting, visible wrapped around the stone and attached to the wooden tripod.

Ferns.description: Green ferns growing around the base of the stone basin and lantern, adding soft texture and natural detail to the garden.

Reflective black tiles.description: Black, glossy tiles arranged in a grid pattern in the Tsuboniwa, reflecting the sky, trees, and surrounding elements, creating a modern and serene visual effect, described by narration as 'polished paving stones' that reflect the maple leaves.

Okuno-in.description: A temple with a traditional thatched roof made of hinoki bark, shown in a still photograph credited to the Kyoto Prefectural Board of Education, nestled among trees, which the narrator explicitly states was re-thatched in 2014.

Blue plastic tub.description: A large, round blue plastic container filled with water, used to soak bundles of hinoki bark before installation, shown with hands submerging and lifting the bark, accompanied by distinct sounds of water splashing and sloshing.

Scaffolding structure.description: A wooden framework of poles and planks supporting the roof during the thatching process, providing access for the worker to reach the roof surface.

Hinoki-bark thatching.description: The traditional roofing technique using overlapping strips of hinoki bark, shown in both completed and under-construction states, with close-ups of the texture and installation process; the narrator refers to these as 'Hinoki cypress bark strips' that are submerged in water to make them pliable and layered to fit the roof's contours.

Kiyomizu-dera.description: A historic wooden temple with a dark, layered hinoki-bark roof, perched on a hillside overlooking Kyoto, surrounded by dense greenery, with visitors visible on its veranda, whose name is mentioned by the narrator in relation to its Okuno-in temple.

Worker in blue uniform and white hard ha.description: A craftsman wearing a blue work uniform, white hard hat, safety goggles, and a watch, actively installing hinoki bark on a roof under scaffolding, demonstrating precision and focus, referred to by the narrator as 'The thatcher' who puts 20 to 30 pegs in his mouth at a time.

Bamboo pegs.description: Small, light-brown wooden pegs used to secure the hinoki bark to the roof structure, shown in a pile and being inserted into the bark layers, which the narrator confirms are used for securing the bark, noting that the thatcher holds 20 to 30 in his mouth at a time.

English narration explains that the maple, positioned behind the basin, enhances the garden's height. Gentle, traditional Japanese music begins as the scene transitions to the completed Tsuboniwa, now featuring the stone basin, a stone lantern, a small maple tree, and black pebbles arranged around reflective black tiles. The narration describes the Tsuboniwa as an ancient art, preserved by generations of landscape artists, and Inoue's revamped garden as a masterpiece of skill and soul, noting how the maple leaves reflect off the polished paving stones, giving the space further depth. Landscape artist Inoue Katsuhiro, seen beside his serene garden, then speaks in Japanese, explaining his philosophy of preserving traditional elements while incorporating new ones, adding his own feelings to what has been taught since ancient times. The camera pans across the tranquil garden, highlighting the reflective tiles and moss-covered stones, as the music continues. The segment concludes with a sweeping aerial view of Kyoto, showcasing lush greenery and traditional architecture, accompanied by a bird chirping and a transition sound, with on-screen text indicating 'Hinoki bark thatching.'

The segment opens with sweeping aerial views of Kiyomizu-dera, a historic wooden temple with its iconic dark, layered hinoki-bark thatched roof, perched on a hillside overlooking Kyoto amidst lush greenery, all set to a calm, traditional Japanese-style background music featuring string instruments and flutes. On-screen text identifies the 'Hinoki-bark thatching' and 'Kiyomizu-dera'. The scene transitions to a still image of Okuno-in, another temple with a similar thatched roof, as a narrator explains that "The roof of Kiyomizu-dera's Okuno-in was re-thatched in 2014." The focus then shifts to the active restoration of a roof under scaffolding, where a worker in a blue uniform and white hard hat is seen carefully placing bundles of hinoki bark. Close-ups show the worker's hands soaking the bark in a blue plastic tub, accompanied by distinct sounds of water splashing and sloshing, as the narrator details that "Hinoki cypress bark strips are first submerged in water to make them pliable and easy to handle." The worker meticulously layers and secures the bark on the roof, with rhythmic tapping sounds accompanying the action, as the narrator adds that "The strips are layered to fit the roof's contours." Further close-ups reveal the worker securing the bark with bamboo pegs, with the narrator stating, "They are then secured with bamboo pegs." event_blocks.summary: Landscape artist Kitayama Yasuo, initially by a forest stream in a blue and white striped shirt and beige pants, explained in Japanese the critical importance of visualizing and assessing stones for garden design. A serene Karesansui garden at Cho’ontei (Kennin-ji) was then presented with contemplative background music, identified by an English narrator who noted Kitayama discovered the central rock in the mountains. The camera panned across the moss-covered courtyard, highlighting the natural textures and composition of the rocks, particularly the Sanzonseki (Buddhist triad rocks) representing Buddha and two Bodhisattvas, arranged for optimal viewing from any angle. Close-ups of the rugged rock surfaces underscored the narrator's conclusion of Buddha's omnipresence. Kitayama Yasuo, now wearing a red jacket over his striped shirt, directly addressed the camera in Japanese, asserting stones are honest and their power is revealed through proper arrangement. A subsequent garden featured a dense rock cluster, with on-screen text suggesting such arrangements invigorate visitors. The video concluded with a view from a traditional Japanese room, featuring tatami flooring and shoji screens, looking onto a garden, where Kitayama Yasuo stressed conveying the stones' power to visitors, all under natural, bright lighting with sunlight filtering through trees, maintaining a peaceful and contemplative mood.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a serene view of a Karesansui garden, accompanied by a Japanese voiceover discussing the "life" of stones, before transitioning to an outdoor construction site. Plasterer Tanaka Akiyoshi, clad in a gray long-sleeve shirt, beige pants, and black rubber boots, is introduced as following in his father's footsteps. The sound of mixing accompanies Tanaka as he prepares a muddy plaster mixture in a large tray and applies it to a wooden-framed wall, smoothing it with a trowel. An English narrator explains Tanaka's pursuit of his ideal wall-crafting, panning across a completed traditional Kyoto-style building in Gion featuring a peach-colored Juraku wall with latticed windows. Tanaka expresses his awe at its beauty, which the narrator elaborates on, noting Juraku walls are made with the best soil and that Tanaka was struck by their color and texture a decade prior. In a workshop, Tanaka is seen mixing plaster again, aiming to use premium soil for his dream walls. He then meticulously applies a thick, wet, coarse, fibrous, traditional earthen plaster, identified as a "clay mix with slightly rough straw as the base," to a wooden-framed interior wall, smoothing it from top to bottom with a trowel. The narrator details the "final step" involves applying the plaster in two stages: a "first coat" of "lighter colored, clay-like mixture" acting as a "canvas," followed by a "second coat" of finer, sand-like plaster applied before the first dries. Tanaka refines this final coat with a small tool for a uniform, slightly textured finish, working with "great care" to achieve the desired "soil" character. On-screen text labels 'Karesansui gardens', 'Kyoto walls', 'Tanaka Akiyoshi Plasterer', and 'Juraku wall' appear, alongside the 'NHK WORLD JAPAN' logo and 'Core Kyoto Special'.

event_blocks.summary: Gary Tegler, seated in a traditional tatami room, introduces the 'Core Kyoto Special' segment, highlighting Kyoto's dedication to detail and the generational transmission of supreme artisan crafts, accompanied by gentle traditional Japanese music. The narrative shifts to visual demonstrations of these crafts: a wooden loom with colorful threads, a fine brush applying gold thread to fabric using the Kirikane technique, and the intricate adornment of a large, carved wooden Miroku Bosatsu statue with gold patterns on its robe. Gary Tegler remarks on the awe-inspiring nature of witnessing these artisans and their deep traditions. The scene then moves to a chef in a white uniform handling raw meat, followed by workers in blue attire and a man in a dark jacket using ropes and a wooden tripod to carefully lower a moss-covered stone water basin into a Tsuboniwa garden. A craftsman sands a wooden beam, while another applies earthen plaster to a wall. Gary Tegler elaborates that mastery demands patience, and the repetition, when performed by descendants of master artisans, gains gravitas and represents Kyoto's finest offerings. The segment concludes with Gary Tegler, on-screen credits for Director Yamasaki Daisuke and Executive Producers Hoshii Wataru and Sugiyama Takashi, a title card for 'Core Kyoto Special' produced by NHK 2023 in association with NHK ENTERPRISES and GENKI Production, and the NHK WORLD JAPAN logo, with the NHK WORLD JAPAN logo and 'Core Kyoto Special' text consistently present in the top left corner.

raw_segments: The segment opens with Gary Tegler, a man with gray hair and glasses in a dark plaid shirt, seated at a red table in a traditional tatami room with shoji screens, a lit lantern, and a hanging scroll, introducing the 'Core Kyoto Special' segment. He speaks in a clear, articulate voice about Kyoto's attention to detail and how supreme artisans share their craft, noting that remarkable levels of skill have been handed down in families for generations, all set to gentle, traditional Japanese background music. The scene transitions to a close-up of a wooden loom with colorful threads arranged radially, followed by a fine brush applying gold thread to fabric using the Kirikane technique, and a large, intricately carved wooden Miroku Bosatsu statue being adorned with delicate gold patterns on its robe. Gary continues, emphasizing that seeing these artisans in action evokes awe and a sense of deep traditions. We then see a chef in a white uniform handling raw meat in a kitchen, followed by workers in blue work attire and a man in a dark jacket using ropes and a wooden tripod to carefully lower a large, moss-covered stone water basin into a serene Tsuboniwa garden. A craftsman in a white hard hat sands a wooden beam, and another applies traditional earthen plaster to a wall, as Gary explains that becoming a master requires patience, and while the repetition might seem like drudgery, when it's done by the great granddaughter or great grandson of a master weaver, dyer, or painter, it exudes gravitas and is genuine, representing the best of what Kyoto presents to the world. The video returns to Gary Tegler, now with on-screen credits for the production team, including Director Yamasaki Daisuke and Executive Producers Hoshii Wataru and Sugiyama Takashi. The segment concludes with a title card for 'Core Kyoto Special,' produced by NHK 2023 in association with NHK ENTERPRISES and GENKI Production, followed by the NHK WORLD JAPAN logo. Throughout, the NHK WORLD JAPAN logo and 'Core Kyoto Special' text remain in the top left corner.
